answer:
In *Traité de Législation* (Vol. I), chapters 8 and 9 form a conceptual pair critiquing non-empirical foundations for morality and legislation—specifically, systems that derive duties, laws, and moral precepts exclusively from the dogmas or "precepts contained in this or that book" (i.e., positive religious scripture or revelation) interpreted by its ministers, rather than from observation of facts and their real-world causes/effects.[^1][^2] 

Comte's cumulative claim across the chapters is that such religiously dogmatic systems are **human inventions unsupported by any actual religious precept**, which systematically **stifle civilizational progress** by excluding rational inquiry into the consequences of actions, habits, and institutions; by **arresting nations in barbarism or stagnation** (as seen in much of Asia, Africa, and parts of Europe); by fostering **hypocrisy, priestly dominance over magistrates, and endless sectarian strife**; and by rendering morality **perishable and selective** (e.g., unbelievers or rival sects need not follow "social virtues" like property respect if the foundation is a rejected dogma).[^3][^4][^5] 

**The positive alternative**—which ch. 9 reinforces by critiquing even "utility" as an a priori *duty* rather than a derived fact—is to ground legislation purely in **empirical analysis** of what causes human/national prosperity vs. decline (via the "analytical method"), trusting the "indestructible tendency" of peoples toward self-betterment once enlightened by facts.[^6][^7] This method has "no other force than that which belongs to truth," welcoming all insights (religious or otherwise) that align with observed reality.[^8]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 8, p-89a0f7](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-89a0f7).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 8, p-a28373](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-a28373).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 8](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/).
[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 8, p-e96a25](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-e96a25).
[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 8, p-6fa397](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-6fa397).
[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 9](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/).
[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 9, p-45fc4e](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/09-de-la-doctrine-qui-fonde-la-morale-et-la-legislati/translation/#p-45fc4e).
[^8]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 8, p-d59d51](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-d59d51).
